To her he thinks not worthy, yet he wooes,
Yet will he swear he loves.

speeches.speech: Now, pray thee, come; Or, if thou wilt hold longer argument, Do it in notes.
lines: Now, pray thee, come;
Or, if thou wilt hold longer argument,
Do it in notes.

speeches.speech: Note this before my notes; There's not a note of mine that's worth the noting.
lines: Note this before my notes;
There's not a note of mine that's worth the noting.

speeches.speech: Why, these are very crotchets that he speaks; Note, notes, forsooth, and nothing.
lines: Why, these are very crotchets that he speaks;
Note, notes, forsooth, and nothing.

speeches.speech: Now, divine air! now is his soul ravished! Is it not strange that sheeps' guts should hale souls out of men's bodies? Well, a horn for my money, when all's done.
lines: Now, divine air! now is his soul ravished! Is it
not strange that sheeps' guts should hale souls out
of men's bodies? Well, a horn for my money, when
all's done.

speeches.speech: Sigh no more, ladies, sigh no more, Men were deceivers ever, One foot in sea and one on shore, To one thing constant never: Then sigh not so, but let them go, And be you blithe and bonny, Converting all your sounds of woe Into Hey nonny, nonny. Sing no more ditties, sing no moe, Of dumps so dull and heavy; The fraud of men was ever so, Since summer first was leafy: Then sigh not so, &c.
lines: Sigh no more, ladies, sigh no more,
Men were deceivers ever,
One foot in sea and one on shore,
To one thing constant never:
Then sigh not so, but let them go,
And be you blithe and bonny,
Converting all your sounds of woe
Into Hey nonny, nonny.
Sing no more ditties, sing no moe,
Of dumps so dull and heavy;
The fraud of men was ever so,
Since summer first was leafy:
Then sigh not so, &c.

speeches.speech: By my troth, a good song.
lines: By my troth, a good song.

speeches.speech: And an ill singer, my lord.
lines: And an ill singer, my lord.

speeches.speech: Ha, no, no, faith; thou singest well enough for a shift.
lines: Ha, no, no, faith; thou singest well enough for a shift.

speeches.speech: An he had been a dog that should have howled thus, they would have hanged him: and I pray God his bad voice bode no mischief. I had as lief have heard the night-raven, come what plague could have come after it.
lines: An he had been a dog that should have howled thus,
they would have hanged him: and I pray God his bad
voice bode no mischief. I had as lief have heard the
night-raven, come what plague could have come after
it.

speeches.speech: Yea, marry, dost thou hear, Balthasar? I pray thee, get us some excellent music; for to-morrow night we would have it at the Lady Hero's chamber-window.
lines: Yea, marry, dost thou hear, Balthasar? I pray thee,
get us some excellent music; for to-morrow night we
would have it at the Lady Hero's chamber-window.

speeches.speech: The best I can, my lord.
lines: The best I can, my lord.

speeches.speech: Do so: farewell. [Exit BALTHASAR] Come hither, Leonato. What was it you told me of to-day, that your niece Beatrice was in love with Signior Benedick?
lines: Do so: farewell.
[Exit BALTHASAR]
Come hither, Leonato. What was it you told me of
to-day, that your niece Beatrice was in love with
Signior Benedick?

speeches.speech: O, ay: stalk on. stalk on; the fowl sits. I did never think that lady would have loved any man.
lines: O, ay: stalk on. stalk on; the fowl sits. I did
never think that lady would have loved any man.

speeches.speech: No, nor I neither; but most wonderful that she should so dote on Signior Benedick, whom she hath in all outward behaviors seemed ever to abhor.
lines: No, nor I neither; but most wonderful that she
should so dote on Signior Benedick, whom she hath in
all outward behaviors seemed ever to abhor.

speeches.speech: Is't possible? Sits the wind in that corner?
lines: Is't possible? Sits the wind in that corner?

speeches.speech: By my troth, my lord, I cannot tell what to think of it but that she loves him with an enraged affection: it is past the infinite of thought.
lines: By my troth, my lord, I cannot tell what to think
of it but that she loves him with an enraged
affection: it is past the infinite of thought.

speeches.speech: May be she doth but counterfeit.
lines: May be she doth but counterfeit.

speeches.speech: Faith, like enough.
lines: Faith, like enough.

speeches.speech: O God, counterfeit! There was never counterfeit of passion came so near the life of passion as she discovers it.
lines: O God, counterfeit! There was never counterfeit of
passion came so near the life of passion as she
discovers it.

speeches.speech: Why, what effects of passion shows she?
lines: Why, what effects of passion shows she?

speeches.speech: Bait the hook well; this fish will bite.
lines: Bait the hook well; this fish will bite.

speeches.speech: What effects, my lord? She will sit you, you heard my daughter tell you how.
lines: What effects, my lord? She will sit you, you heard
my daughter tell you how.

speeches.speech: She did, indeed.
lines: She did, indeed.

speeches.speech: How, how, pray you? You amaze me: I would have I thought her spirit had been invincible against all assaults of affection.
lines: How, how, pray you? You amaze me: I would have I
thought her spirit had been invincible against all
assaults of affection.

speeches.speech: I would have sworn it had, my lord; especially against Benedick.
lines: I would have sworn it had, my lord; especially
against Benedick.

speeches.speech: I should think this a gull, but that the white-bearded fellow speaks it: knavery cannot, sure, hide himself in such reverence.
lines: I should think this a gull, but that the
white-bearded fellow speaks it: knavery cannot,
sure, hide himself in such reverence.

speeches.speech: He hath ta'en the infection: hold it up.
lines: He hath ta'en the infection: hold it up.

speeches.speech: Hath she made her affection known to Benedick?
lines: Hath she made her affection known to Benedick?

speeches.speech: No; and swears she never will: that's her torment.
lines: No; and swears she never will: that's her torment.

speeches.speech: 'Tis true, indeed; so your daughter says: 'Shall I,' says she, 'that have so oft encountered him with scorn, write to him that I love him?'
lines: 'Tis true, indeed; so your daughter says: 'Shall
I,' says she, 'that have so oft encountered him
with scorn, write to him that I love him?'

speeches.speech: This says she now when she is beginning to write to him; for she'll be up twenty times a night, and there will she sit in her smock till she have writ a sheet of paper: my daughter tells us all.
lines: This says she now when she is beginning to write to
him; for she'll be up twenty times a night, and
there will she sit in her smock till she have writ a
sheet of paper: my daughter tells us all.

speeches.speech: Now you talk of a sheet of paper, I remember a pretty jest your daughter told us of.
lines: Now you talk of a sheet of paper, I remember a
pretty jest your daughter told us of.

speeches.speech: O, when she had writ it and was reading it over, she found Benedick and Beatrice between the sheet?
lines: O, when she had writ it and was reading it over, she
found Benedick and Beatrice between the sheet?

speeches.speech: That.
lines: That.

speeches.speech: O, she tore the letter into a thousand halfpence; railed at herself, that she should be so immodest to write to one that she knew would flout her; 'I measure him,' says she, 'by my own spirit; for I should flout him, if he writ to me; yea, though I love him, I should.'
lines: O, she tore the letter into a thousand halfpence;
railed at herself, that she should be so immodest
to write to one that she knew would flout her; 'I
measure him,' says she, 'by my own spirit; for I
should flout him, if he writ to me; yea, though I
love him, I should.'

speeches.speech: Then down upon her knees she falls, weeps, sobs, beats her heart, tears her hair, prays, curses; 'O sweet Benedick! God give me patience!'
lines: Then down upon her knees she falls, weeps, sobs,
beats her heart, tears her hair, prays, curses; 'O
sweet Benedick! God give me patience!'

speeches.speech: She doth indeed; my daughter says so: and the ecstasy hath so much overborne her that my daughter is sometime afeared she will do a desperate outrage to herself: it is very true.
lines: She doth indeed; my daughter says so: and the
ecstasy hath so much overborne her that my daughter
is sometime afeared she will do a desperate outrage
to herself: it is very true.

speeches.speech: It were good that Benedick knew of it by some other, if she will not discover it.
lines: It were good that Benedick knew of it by some
other, if she will not discover it.

speeches.speech: To what end? He would make but a sport of it and torment the poor lady worse.
lines: To what end? He would make but a sport of it and
torment the poor lady worse.

speeches.speech: An he should, it were an alms to hang him. She's an excellent sweet lady; and, out of all suspicion, she is virtuous.
lines: An he should, it were an alms to hang him. She's an
excellent sweet lady; and, out of all suspicion,
she is virtuous.

speeches.speech: And she is exceeding wise.
lines: And she is exceeding wise.

speeches.speech: In every thing but in loving Benedick.
lines: In every thing but in loving Benedick.

speeches.speech: O, my lord, wisdom and blood combating in so tender a body, we have ten proofs to one that blood hath the victory. I am sorry for her, as I have just cause, being her uncle and her guardian.
lines: O, my lord, wisdom and blood combating in so tender
a body, we have ten proofs to one that blood hath
the victory. I am sorry for her, as I have just
cause, being her uncle and her guardian.

speeches.speech: I would she had bestowed this dotage on me: I would have daffed all other respects and made her half myself. I pray you, tell Benedick of it, and hear what a' will say.
lines: I would she had bestowed this dotage on me: I would
have daffed all other respects and made her half
myself. I pray you, tell Benedick of it, and hear
what a' will say.

speeches.speech: Were it good, think you?
lines: Were it good, think you?

speeches.speech: Hero thinks surely she will die; for she says she will die, if he love her not, and she will die, ere she make her love known, and she will die, if he woo her, rather than she will bate one breath of her accustomed crossness.
lines: Hero thinks surely she will die; for she says she
will die, if he love her not, and she will die, ere
she make her love known, and she will die, if he woo
her, rather than she will bate one breath of her
accustomed crossness.

speeches.speech: She doth well: if she should make tender of her love, 'tis very possible he'll scorn it; for the man, as you know all, hath a contemptible spirit.
lines: She doth well: if she should make tender of her
love, 'tis very possible he'll scorn it; for the
man, as you know all, hath a contemptible spirit.

speeches.speech: He is a very proper man.
lines: He is a very proper man.

speeches.speech: He hath indeed a good outward happiness.
lines: He hath indeed a good outward happiness.

speeches.speech: Before God! and, in my mind, very wise.
lines: Before God! and, in my mind, very wise.

speeches.speech: He doth indeed show some sparks that are like wit.
lines: He doth indeed show some sparks that are like wit.

speeches.speech: And I take him to be valiant.
lines: And I take him to be valiant.

speeches.speech: As Hector, I assure you: and in the managing of quarrels you may say he is wise; for either he avoids them with great discretion, or undertakes them with a most Christian-like fear.
lines: As Hector, I assure you: and in the managing of
quarrels you may say he is wise; for either he
avoids them with great discretion, or undertakes
them with a most Christian-like fear.

speeches.speech: If he do fear God, a' must necessarily keep peace: if he break the peace, he ought to enter into a quarrel with fear and trembling.
lines: If he do fear God, a' must necessarily keep peace:
if he break the peace, he ought to enter into a
quarrel with fear and trembling.

speeches.speech: And so will he do; for the man doth fear God, howsoever it seems not in him by some large jests he will make. Well I am sorry for your niece. Shall we go seek Benedick, and tell him of her love?
lines: And so will he do; for the man doth fear God,
howsoever it seems not in him by some large jests
he will make. Well I am sorry for your niece. Shall
we go seek Benedick, and tell him of her love?

speeches.speech: Never tell him, my lord: let her wear it out with good counsel.
lines: Never tell him, my lord: let her wear it out with
good counsel.

speeches.speech: Nay, that's impossible: she may wear her heart out first.
lines: Nay, that's impossible: she may wear her heart out first.

speeches.speech: Well, we will hear further of it by your daughter: let it cool the while. I love Benedick well; and I could wish he would modestly examine himself, to see how much he is unworthy so good a lady.
lines: Well, we will hear further of it by your daughter:
let it cool the while. I love Benedick well; and I
could wish he would modestly examine himself, to see
how much he is unworthy so good a lady.

speeches.speech: My lord, will you walk? dinner is ready.
lines: My lord, will you walk? dinner is ready.

speeches.speech: If he do not dote on her upon this, I will never trust my expectation.
lines: If he do not dote on her upon this, I will never
trust my expectation.

speeches.speech: Let there be the same net spread for her; and that must your daughter and her gentlewomen carry. The sport will be, when they hold one an opinion of another's dotage, and no such matter: that's the scene that I would see, which will be merely a dumb-show. Let us send her to call him in to dinner.
lines: Let there be the same net spread for her; and that
must your daughter and her gentlewomen carry. The
sport will be, when they hold one an opinion of
another's dotage, and no such matter: that's the
scene that I would see, which will be merely a
dumb-show. Let us send her to call him in to dinner.

speeches.speech: [Coming forward] This can be no trick: the conference was sadly borne. They have the truth of this from Hero. They seem to pity the lady: it seems her affections have their full bent. Love me! why, it must be requited. I hear how I am censured: they say I will bear myself proudly, if I perceive the love come from her; they say too that she will rather die than give any sign of affection. I did never think to marry: I must not seem proud: happy are they that hear their detractions and can put them to mending. They say the lady is fair; 'tis a truth, I can bear them witness; and virtuous; 'tis so, I cannot reprove it; and wise, but for loving me; by my troth, it is no addition to her wit, nor no great argument of her folly, for I will be horribly in love with her. I may chance have some odd quirks and remnants of wit broken on me, because I have railed so long against marriage: but doth not the appetite alter? a man loves the meat in his youth that he cannot endure in his age. Shall quips and sentences and these paper bullets of the brain awe a man from the career of his humour? No, the world must be peopled. When I said I would die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till I were married. Here comes Beatrice. By this day! she's a fair lady: I do spy some marks of love in her.
lines: [Coming forward] This can be no trick: the
conference was sadly borne. They have the truth of
this from Hero. They seem to pity the lady: it
seems her affections have their full bent. Love me!
why, it must be requited. I hear how I am censured:
they say I will bear myself proudly, if I perceive
the love come from her; they say too that she will
rather die than give any sign of affection. I did
never think to marry: I must not seem proud: happy
are they that hear their detractions and can put
them to mending. They say the lady is fair; 'tis a
truth, I can bear them witness; and virtuous; 'tis
so, I cannot reprove it; and wise, but for loving
me; by my troth, it is no addition to her wit, nor
no great argument of her folly, for I will be
horribly in love with her. I may chance have some
odd quirks and remnants of wit broken on me,
because I have railed so long against marriage: but
doth not the appetite alter? a man loves the meat
in his youth that he cannot endure in his age.
Shall quips and sentences and these paper bullets of
the brain awe a man from the career of his humour?
No, the world must be peopled. When I said I would
die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till I
were married. Here comes Beatrice. By this day!
she's a fair lady: I do spy some marks of love in
her.

speeches.speech: Against my will I am sent to bid you come in to dinner.
lines: Against my will I am sent to bid you come in to dinner.

speeches.speech: Fair Beatrice, I thank you for your pains.
lines: Fair Beatrice, I thank you for your pains.

speeches.speech: I took no more pains for those thanks than you take pains to thank me: if it had been painful, I would not have come.
lines: I took no more pains for those thanks than you take
pains to thank me: if it had been painful, I would
not have come.

speeches.speech: You take pleasure then in the message?
lines: You take pleasure then in the message?

speeches.speech: Yea, just so much as you may take upon a knife's point and choke a daw withal. You have no stomach, signior: fare you well.
lines: Yea, just so much as you may take upon a knife's
point and choke a daw withal. You have no stomach,
signior: fare you well.

speeches.speech: Ha! 'Against my will I am sent to bid you come in to dinner;' there's a double meaning in that 'I took no more pains for those thanks than you took pains to thank me.' that's as much as to say, Any pains that I take for you is as easy as thanks. If I do not take pity of her, I am a villain; if I do not love her, I am a Jew. I will go get her picture.
lines: Ha! 'Against my will I am sent to bid you come in
to dinner;' there's a double meaning in that 'I took
no more pains for those thanks than you took pains
to thank me.' that's as much as to say, Any pains
that I take for you is as easy as thanks. If I do
not take pity of her, I am a villain; if I do not
love her, I am a Jew. I will go get her picture.

speeches.speech: Good Margaret, run thee to the parlor; There shalt thou find my cousin Beatrice Proposing with the prince and Claudio: Whisper her ear and tell her, I and Ursula Walk in the orchard and our whole discourse Is all of her; say that thou overheard'st us; And bid her steal into the pleached bower, Where honeysuckles, ripen'd by the sun, Forbid the sun to enter, like favourites, Made proud by princes, that advance their pride Against that power that bred it: there will she hide her, To listen our purpose. This is thy office; Bear thee well in it and leave us alone.
lines: Good Margaret, run thee to the parlor;
There shalt thou find my cousin Beatrice
Proposing with the prince and Claudio:
Whisper her ear and tell her, I and Ursula
Walk in the orchard and our whole discourse
Is all of her; say that thou overheard'st us;
And bid her steal into the pleached bower,
Where honeysuckles, ripen'd by the sun,
Forbid the sun to enter, like favourites,
Made proud by princes, that advance their pride
Against that power that bred it: there will she hide her,
To listen our purpose. This is thy office;
Bear thee well in it and leave us alone.

speeches.speech: I'll make her come, I warrant you, presently.
lines: I'll make her come, I warrant you, presently.

speeches.speech: Now, Ursula, when Beatrice doth come, As we do trace this alley up and down, Our talk must only be of Benedick. When I do name him, let it be thy part To praise him more than ever man did merit: My talk to thee must be how Benedick Is sick in love with Beatrice. Of this matter Is little Cupid's crafty arrow made, That only wounds by hearsay. [Enter BEATRICE, behind] Now begin; For look where Beatrice, like a lapwing, runs Close by the ground, to hear our conference.
lines: Now, Ursula, when Beatrice doth come,
As we do trace this alley up and down,
Our talk must only be of Benedick.
When I do name him, let it be thy part
To praise him more than ever man did merit:
My talk to thee must be how Benedick
Is sick in love with Beatrice. Of this matter
Is little Cupid's crafty arrow made,
That only wounds by hearsay.
[Enter BEATRICE, behind]
Now begin;
For look where Beatrice, like a lapwing, runs
Close by the ground, to hear our conference.

speeches.speech: The pleasant'st angling is to see the fish Cut with her golden oars the silver stream, And greedily devour the treacherous bait: So angle we for Beatrice; who even now Is couched in the woodbine coverture. Fear you not my part of the dialogue.
lines: The pleasant'st angling is to see the fish
Cut with her golden oars the silver stream,
And greedily devour the treacherous bait:
So angle we for Beatrice; who even now
Is couched in the woodbine coverture.
Fear you not my part of the dialogue.

speeches.speech: Then go we near her, that her ear lose nothing Of the false sweet bait that we lay for it. [Approaching the bower] No, truly, Ursula, she is too disdainful; I know her spirits are as coy and wild As haggerds of the rock.
lines: Then go we near her, that her ear lose nothing
Of the false sweet bait that we lay for it.
[Approaching the bower]
No, truly, Ursula, she is too disdainful;
I know her spirits are as coy and wild
As haggerds of the rock.

speeches.speech: But are you sure That Benedick loves Beatrice so entirely?
lines: But are you sure
That Benedick loves Beatrice so entirely?

speeches.speech: So says the prince and my new-trothed lord.
lines: So says the prince and my new-trothed lord.

speeches.speech: And did they bid you tell her of it, madam?
lines: And did they bid you tell her of it, madam?

speeches.speech: They did entreat me to acquaint her of it; But I persuaded them, if they loved Benedick, To wish him wrestle with affection, And never to let Beatrice know of it.
lines: They did entreat me to acquaint her of it;
But I persuaded them, if they loved Benedick,
To wish him wrestle with affection,
And never to let Beatrice know of it.

speeches.speech: Why did you so? Doth not the gentleman Deserve as full as fortunate a bed As ever Beatrice shall couch upon?
lines: Why did you so? Doth not the gentleman
Deserve as full as fortunate a bed
As ever Beatrice shall couch upon?

speeches.speech: O god of love! I know he doth deserve As much as may be yielded to a man: But Nature never framed a woman's heart Of prouder stuff than that of Beatrice; Disdain and scorn ride sparkling in her eyes, Misprising what they look on, and her wit Values itself so highly that to her All matter else seems weak: she cannot love, Nor take no shape nor project of affection, She is so self-endeared.
lines: O god of love! I know he doth deserve
As much as may be yielded to a man:
But Nature never framed a woman's heart
Of prouder stuff than that of Beatrice;
Disdain and scorn ride sparkling in her eyes,
Misprising what they look on, and her wit
Values itself so highly that to her
All matter else seems weak: she cannot love,
Nor take no shape nor project of affection,
She is so self-endeared.

speeches.speech: Sure, I think so; And therefore certainly it were not good She knew his love, lest she make sport at it.
lines: Sure, I think so;
And therefore certainly it were not good
She knew his love, lest she make sport at it.

speeches.speech: Why, you speak truth. I never yet saw man, How wise, how noble, young, how rarely featured, But she would spell him backward: if fair-faced, She would swear the gentleman should be her sister; If black, why, Nature, drawing of an antique, Made a foul blot; if tall, a lance ill-headed; If low, an agate very vilely cut; If speaking, why, a vane blown with all winds; If silent, why, a block moved with none. So turns she every man the wrong side out And never gives to truth and virtue that Which simpleness and merit purchaseth.
lines: Why, you speak truth. I never yet saw man,
How wise, how noble, young, how rarely featured,
But she would spell him backward: if fair-faced,
She would swear the gentleman should be her sister;
If black, why, Nature, drawing of an antique,
Made a foul blot; if tall, a lance ill-headed;
If low, an agate very vilely cut;
If speaking, why, a vane blown with all winds;
If silent, why, a block moved with none.
So turns she every man the wrong side out
And never gives to truth and virtue that
Which simpleness and merit purchaseth.

speeches.speech: Sure, sure, such carping is not commendable.
lines: Sure, sure, such carping is not commendable.

speeches.speech: No, not to be so odd and from all fashions As Beatrice is, cannot be commendable: But who dare tell her so? If I should speak, She would mock me into air; O, she would laugh me Out of myself, press me to death with wit. Therefore let Benedick, like cover'd fire, Consume away in sighs, waste inwardly: It were a better death than die with mocks, Which is as bad as die with tickling.
lines: No, not to be so odd and from all fashions
As Beatrice is, cannot be commendable:
But who dare tell her so? If I should speak,
She would mock me into air; O, she would laugh me
Out of myself, press me to death with wit.
Therefore let Benedick, like cover'd fire,
Consume away in sighs, waste inwardly:
It were a better death than die with mocks,
Which is as bad as die with tickling.

speeches.speech: Yet tell her of it: hear what she will say.
lines: Yet tell her of it: hear what she will say.

speeches.speech: No; rather I will go to Benedick And counsel him to fight against his passion. And, truly, I'll devise some honest slanders To stain my cousin with: one doth not know How much an ill word may empoison liking.
lines: No; rather I will go to Benedick
And counsel him to fight against his passion.
And, truly, I'll devise some honest slanders
To stain my cousin with: one doth not know
How much an ill word may empoison liking.

speeches.speech: O, do not do your cousin such a wrong. She cannot be so much without true judgment-- Having so swift and excellent a wit As she is prized to have--as to refuse So rare a gentleman as Signior Benedick.
lines: O, do not do your cousin such a wrong.
She cannot be so much without true judgment--
Having so swift and excellent a wit
As she is prized to have--as to refuse
So rare a gentleman as Signior Benedick.

speeches.speech: He is the only man of Italy. Always excepted my dear Claudio.
lines: He is the only man of Italy.
Always excepted my dear Claudio.

speeches.speech: I pray you, be not angry with me, madam, Speaking my fancy: Signior Benedick, For shape, for bearing, argument and valour, Goes foremost in report through Italy.
lines: I pray you, be not angry with me, madam,
Speaking my fancy: Signior Benedick,
For shape, for bearing, argument and valour,
Goes foremost in report through Italy.

speeches.speech: Indeed, he hath an excellent good name.
lines: Indeed, he hath an excellent good name.

speeches.speech: His excellence did earn it, ere he had it. When are you married, madam?
lines: His excellence did earn it, ere he had it.
When are you married, madam?

speeches.speech: Why, every day, to-morrow. Come, go in: I'll show thee some attires, and have thy counsel Which is the best to furnish me to-morrow.
lines: Why, every day, to-morrow. Come, go in:
I'll show thee some attires, and have thy counsel
Which is the best to furnish me to-morrow.

speeches.speech: She's limed, I warrant you: we have caught her, madam.
lines: She's limed, I warrant you: we have caught her, madam.

speeches.speech: If it proves so, then loving goes by haps: Some Cupid kills with arrows, some with traps.
lines: If it proves so, then loving goes by haps:
Some Cupid kills with arrows, some with traps.

speeches.speech: [Coming forward] What fire is in mine ears? Can this be true? Stand I condemn'd for pride and scorn so much? Contempt, farewell! and maiden pride, adieu! No glory lives behind the back of such. And, Benedick, love on; I will requite thee, Taming my wild heart to thy loving hand: If thou dost love, my kindness shall incite thee To bind our loves up in a holy band; For others say thou dost deserve, and I Believe it better than reportingly.
lines: [Coming forward]
What fire is in mine ears? Can this be true?
Stand I condemn'd for pride and scorn so much?
Contempt, farewell! and maiden pride, adieu!
No glory lives behind the back of such.
And, Benedick, love on; I will requite thee,
Taming my wild heart to thy loving hand:
If thou dost love, my kindness shall incite thee
To bind our loves up in a holy band;
For others say thou dost deserve, and I
Believe it better than reportingly.

speeches.speech: I do but stay till your marriage be consummate, and then go I toward Arragon.
lines: I do but stay till your marriage be consummate, and
then go I toward Arragon.

speeches.speech: I'll bring you thither, my lord, if you'll vouchsafe me.
lines: I'll bring you thither, my lord, if you'll
vouchsafe me.

speeches.speech: Nay, that would be as great a soil in the new gloss of your marriage as to show a child his new coat and forbid him to wear it. I will only be bold with Benedick for his company; for, from the crown of his head to the sole of his foot, he is all mirth: he hath twice or thrice cut Cupid's bow-string and the little hangman dare not shoot at him; he hath a heart as sound as a bell and his tongue is the clapper, for what his heart thinks his tongue speaks.
lines: Nay, that would be as great a soil in the new gloss
of your marriage as to show a child his new coat
and forbid him to wear it. I will only be bold
with Benedick for his company; for, from the crown
of his head to the sole of his foot, he is all
mirth: he hath twice or thrice cut Cupid's
bow-string and the little hangman dare not shoot at
him; he hath a heart as sound as a bell and his
tongue is the clapper, for what his heart thinks his
tongue speaks.

speeches.speech: Gallants, I am not as I have been.
lines: Gallants, I am not as I have been.

speeches.speech: So say I. methinks you are sadder.
lines: So say I. methinks you are sadder.

speeches.speech: I hope he be in love.
lines: I hope he be in love.

speeches.speech: Hang him, truant! there's no true drop of blood in him, to be truly touched with love: if he be sad, he wants money.
lines: Hang him, truant! there's no true drop of blood in
him, to be truly touched with love: if he be sad,
he wants money.

speeches.speech: I have the toothache.
lines: I have the toothache.

speeches.speech: Draw it.
lines: Draw it.

speeches.speech: Hang it!
lines: Hang it!

speeches.speech: You must hang it first, and draw it afterwards.
lines: You must hang it first, and draw it afterwards.

speeches.speech: What! sigh for the toothache?
lines: What! sigh for the toothache?

speeches.speech: Where is but a humour or a worm.
lines: Where is but a humour or a worm.

speeches.speech: Well, every one can master a grief but he that has it.
lines: Well, every one can master a grief but he that has
it.

speeches.speech: Yet say I, he is in love.
lines: Yet say I, he is in love.

speeches.speech: There is no appearance of fancy in him, unless it be a fancy that he hath to strange disguises; as, to be a Dutchman today, a Frenchman to-morrow, or in the shape of two countries at once, as, a German from the waist downward, all slops, and a Spaniard from the hip upward, no doublet. Unless he have a fancy to this foolery, as it appears he hath, he is no fool for fancy, as you would have it appear he is.
lines: There is no appearance of fancy in him, unless it be
a fancy that he hath to strange disguises; as, to be
a Dutchman today, a Frenchman to-morrow, or in the
shape of two countries at once, as, a German from
the waist downward, all slops, and a Spaniard from
the hip upward, no doublet. Unless he have a fancy
to this foolery, as it appears he hath, he is no
fool for fancy, as you would have it appear he is.

speeches.speech: If he be not in love with some woman, there is no believing old signs: a' brushes his hat o' mornings; what should that bode?
lines: If he be not in love with some woman, there is no
believing old signs: a' brushes his hat o'
mornings; what should that bode?

speeches.speech: Hath any man seen him at the barber's?
lines: Hath any man seen him at the barber's?

speeches.speech: No, but the barber's man hath been seen with him, and the old ornament of his cheek hath already stuffed tennis-balls.
lines: No, but the barber's man hath been seen with him,
and the old ornament of his cheek hath already
stuffed tennis-balls.

speeches.speech: Indeed, he looks younger than he did, by the loss of a beard.
lines: Indeed, he looks younger than he did, by the loss of a beard.

speeches.speech: Nay, a' rubs himself with civet: can you smell him out by that?
lines: Nay, a' rubs himself with civet: can you smell him
out by that?

speeches.speech: That's as much as to say, the sweet youth's in love.
lines: That's as much as to say, the sweet youth's in love.

speeches.speech: The greatest note of it is his melancholy.
lines: The greatest note of it is his melancholy.

speeches.speech: And when was he wont to wash his face?
lines: And when was he wont to wash his face?

speeches.speech: Yea, or to paint himself? for the which, I hear what they say of him.
lines: Yea, or to paint himself? for the which, I hear
what they say of him.

speeches.speech: Nay, but his jesting spirit; which is now crept into a lute-string and now governed by stops.
lines: Nay, but his jesting spirit; which is now crept into
a lute-string and now governed by stops.

speeches.speech: Indeed, that tells a heavy tale for him: conclude, conclude he is in love.
lines: Indeed, that tells a heavy tale for him: conclude,
conclude he is in love.

speeches.speech: Nay, but I know who loves him.
lines: Nay, but I know who loves him.

speeches.speech: That would I know too: I warrant, one that knows him not.
lines: That would I know too: I warrant, one that knows him not.

speeches.speech: Yes, and his ill conditions; and, in despite of all, dies for him.
lines: Yes, and his ill conditions; and, in despite of
all, dies for him.

speeches.speech: She shall be buried with her face upwards.
lines: She shall be buried with her face upwards.

speeches.speech: Yet is this no charm for the toothache. Old signior, walk aside with me: I have studied eight or nine wise words to speak to you, which these hobby-horses must not hear.
lines: Yet is this no charm for the toothache. Old
signior, walk aside with me: I have studied eight
or nine wise words to speak to you, which these
hobby-horses must not hear.

speeches.speech: For my life, to break with him about Beatrice.
lines: For my life, to break with him about Beatrice.

speeches.speech: 'Tis even so. Hero and Margaret have by this played their parts with Beatrice; and then the two bears will not bite one another when they meet.
lines: 'Tis even so. Hero and Margaret have by this
played their parts with Beatrice; and then the two
bears will not bite one another when they meet.

speeches.speech: My lord and brother, God save you!
lines: My lord and brother, God save you!

speeches.speech: Good den, brother.
lines: Good den, brother.

speeches.speech: If your leisure served, I would speak with you.
lines: If your leisure served, I would speak with you.

speeches.speech: In private?
lines: In private?

speeches.speech: If it please you: yet Count Claudio may hear; for what I would speak of concerns him.
lines: If it please you: yet Count Claudio may hear; for
what I would speak of concerns him.

speeches.speech: What's the matter?
lines: What's the matter?

speeches.speech: [To CLAUDIO] Means your lordship to be married to-morrow?
lines: [To CLAUDIO] Means your lordship to be married
to-morrow?

speeches.speech: You know he does.
lines: You know he does.

speeches.speech: I know not that, when he knows what I know.
lines: I know not that, when he knows what I know.

speeches.speech: If there be any impediment, I pray you discover it.
lines: If there be any impediment, I pray you discover it.

speeches.speech: You may think I love you not: let that appear hereafter, and aim better at me by that I now will manifest. For my brother, I think he holds you well, and in dearness of heart hath holp to effect your ensuing marriage;--surely suit ill spent and labour ill bestowed.
lines: You may think I love you not: let that appear
hereafter, and aim better at me by that I now will
manifest. For my brother, I think he holds you
well, and in dearness of heart hath holp to effect
your ensuing marriage;--surely suit ill spent and
labour ill bestowed.

speeches.speech: Why, what's the matter?
lines: Why, what's the matter?

speeches.speech: I came hither to tell you; and, circumstances shortened, for she has been too long a talking of, the lady is disloyal.
lines: I came hither to tell you; and, circumstances
shortened, for she has been too long a talking of,
the lady is disloyal.

speeches.speech: Who, Hero?
lines: Who, Hero?

speeches.speech: Even she; Leonato's Hero, your Hero, every man's Hero:
lines: Even she; Leonato's Hero, your Hero, every man's Hero:

speeches.speech: Disloyal?
lines: Disloyal?

speeches.speech: The word is too good to paint out her wickedness; I could say she were worse: think you of a worse title, and I will fit her to it. Wonder not till further warrant: go but with me to-night, you shall see her chamber-window entered, even the night before her wedding-day: if you love her then, to-morrow wed her; but it would better fit your honour to change your mind.
lines: The word is too good to paint out her wickedness; I
could say she were worse: think you of a worse
title, and I will fit her to it. Wonder not till
further warrant: go but with me to-night, you shall
see her chamber-window entered, even the night
before her wedding-day: if you love her then,
to-morrow wed her; but it would better fit your honour
to change your mind.

speeches.speech: May this be so?
lines: May this be so?

speeches.speech: I will not think it.
lines: I will not think it.

speeches.speech: If you dare not trust that you see, confess not that you know: if you will follow me, I will show you enough; and when you have seen more and heard more, proceed accordingly.
lines: If you dare not trust that you see, confess not
that you know: if you will follow me, I will show
you enough; and when you have seen more and heard
more, proceed accordingly.

speeches.speech: If I see any thing to-night why I should not marry her to-morrow in the congregation, where I should wed, there will I shame her.
lines: If I see any thing to-night why I should not marry
her to-morrow in the congregation, where I should
wed, there will I shame her.

speeches.speech: And, as I wooed for thee to obtain her, I will join with thee to disgrace her.
lines: And, as I wooed for thee to obtain her, I will join
with thee to disgrace her.

speeches.speech: I will disparage her no farther till you are my witnesses: bear it coldly but till midnight, and let the issue show itself.
lines: I will disparage her no farther till you are my
witnesses: bear it coldly but till midnight, and
let the issue show itself.

speeches.speech: O day untowardly turned!
lines: O day untowardly turned!

speeches.speech: O mischief strangely thwarting!
lines: O mischief strangely thwarting!

speeches.speech: O plague right well prevented! so will you say when you have seen the sequel.
lines: O plague right well prevented! so will you say when
you have seen the sequel.

speeches.speech: Are you good men and true?
lines: Are you good men and true?

speeches.speech: Yea, or else it were pity but they should suffer salvation, body and soul.
lines: Yea, or else it were pity but they should suffer
salvation, body and soul.

speeches.speech: Nay, that were a punishment too good for them, if they should have any allegiance in them, being chosen for the prince's watch.
lines: Nay, that were a punishment too good for them, if
they should have any allegiance in them, being
chosen for the prince's watch.

speeches.speech: Well, give them their charge, neighbour Dogberry.
lines: Well, give them their charge, neighbour Dogberry.

speeches.speech: First, who think you the most desertless man to be constable?
lines: First, who think you the most desertless man to be
constable?

speeches.speech: Hugh Otecake, sir, or George Seacole; for they can write and read.
lines: Hugh Otecake, sir, or George Seacole; for they can
write and read.

speeches.speech: Come hither, neighbour Seacole. God hath blessed you with a good name: to be a well-favoured man is the gift of fortune; but to write and read comes by nature.
lines: Come hither, neighbour Seacole. God hath blessed
you with a good name: to be a well-favoured man is
the gift of fortune; but to write and read comes by nature.

speeches.speech: Both which, master constable,--
lines: Both which, master constable,--

speeches.speech: You have: I knew it would be your answer. Well, for your favour, sir, why, give God thanks, and make no boast of it; and for your writing and reading, let that appear when there is no need of such vanity. You are thought here to be the most senseless and fit man for the constable of the watch; therefore bear you the lantern. This is your charge: you shall comprehend all vagrom men; you are to bid any man stand, in the prince's name.
lines: You have: I knew it would be your answer. Well,
for your favour, sir, why, give God thanks, and make
no boast of it; and for your writing and reading,
let that appear when there is no need of such
vanity. You are thought here to be the most
senseless and fit man for the constable of the
watch; therefore bear you the lantern. This is your
charge: you shall comprehend all vagrom men; you are
to bid any man stand, in the prince's name.

speeches.speech: How if a' will not stand?
lines: How if a' will not stand?

speeches.speech: Why, then, take no note of him, but let him go; and presently call the rest of the watch together and thank God you are rid of a knave.
lines: Why, then, take no note of him, but let him go; and
presently call the rest of the watch together and
thank God you are rid of a knave.

speeches.speech: If he will not stand when he is bidden, he is none of the prince's subjects.
lines: If he will not stand when he is bidden, he is none
of the prince's subjects.

speeches.speech: True, and they are to meddle with none but the prince's subjects. You shall also make no noise in the streets; for, for the watch to babble and to talk is most tolerable and not to be endured.
lines: True, and they are to meddle with none but the
prince's subjects. You shall also make no noise in
the streets; for, for the watch to babble and to
talk is most tolerable and not to be endured.

speeches.speech: We will rather sleep than talk: we know what belongs to a watch.
lines: We will rather sleep than talk: we know what
belongs to a watch.

speeches.speech: Why, you speak like an ancient and most quiet watchman; for I cannot see how sleeping should offend: only, have a care that your bills be not stolen. Well, you are to call at all the ale-houses, and bid those that are drunk get them to bed.
lines: Why, you speak like an ancient and most quiet
watchman; for I cannot see how sleeping should
offend: only, have a care that your bills be not
stolen. Well, you are to call at all the
ale-houses, and bid those that are drunk get them to bed.

speeches.speech: How if they will not?
lines: How if they will not?

speeches.speech: Why, then, let them alone till they are sober: if they make you not then the better answer, you may say they are not the men you took them for.
lines: Why, then, let them alone till they are sober: if
they make you not then the better answer, you may
say they are not the men you took them for.

speeches.speech: Well, sir.
lines: Well, sir.

speeches.speech: If you meet a thief, you may suspect him, by virtue of your office, to be no true man; and, for such kind of men, the less you meddle or make with them, why the more is for your honesty.
lines: If you meet a thief, you may suspect him, by virtue
of your office, to be no true man; and, for such
kind of men, the less you meddle or make with them,
why the more is for your honesty.

speeches.speech: If we know him to be a thief, shall we not lay hands on him?
lines: If we know him to be a thief, shall we not lay
hands on him?

speeches.speech: Truly, by your office, you may; but I think they that touch pitch will be defiled: the most peaceable way for you, if you do take a thief, is to let him show himself what he is and steal out of your company.
lines: Truly, by your office, you may; but I think they
that touch pitch will be defiled: the most peaceable
way for you, if you do take a thief, is to let him
show himself what he is and steal out of your company.

speeches.speech: You have been always called a merciful man, partner.
lines: You have been always called a merciful man, partner.

speeches.speech: Truly, I would not hang a dog by my will, much more a man who hath any honesty in him.
lines: Truly, I would not hang a dog by my will, much more
a man who hath any honesty in him.

speeches.speech: If you hear a child cry in the night, you must call to the nurse and bid her still it.
lines: If you hear a child cry in the night, you must call
to the nurse and bid her still it.

speeches.speech: How if the nurse be asleep and will not hear us?
lines: How if the nurse be asleep and will not hear us?

speeches.speech: Why, then, depart in peace, and let the child wake her with crying; for the ewe that will not hear her lamb when it baes will never answer a calf when he bleats.
lines: Why, then, depart in peace, and let the child wake
her with crying; for the ewe that will not hear her
lamb when it baes will never answer a calf when he bleats.

speeches.speech: 'Tis very true.
lines: 'Tis very true.

speeches.speech: This is the end of the charge:--you, constable, are to present the prince's own person: if you meet the prince in the night, you may stay him.
lines: This is the end of the charge:--you, constable, are
to present the prince's own person: if you meet the
prince in the night, you may stay him.

speeches.speech: Nay, by'r our lady, that I think a' cannot.
lines: Nay, by'r our lady, that I think a' cannot.

speeches.speech: Five shillings to one on't, with any man that knows the statutes, he may stay him: marry, not without the prince be willing; for, indeed, the watch ought to offend no man; and it is an offence to stay a man against his will.
lines: Five shillings to one on't, with any man that knows
the statutes, he may stay him: marry, not without
the prince be willing; for, indeed, the watch ought
to offend no man; and it is an offence to stay a
man against his will.

speeches.speech: By'r lady, I think it be so.
lines: By'r lady, I think it be so.

speeches.speech: Ha, ha, ha! Well, masters, good night: an there be any matter of weight chances, call up me: keep your fellows' counsels and your own; and good night. Come, neighbour.
lines: Ha, ha, ha! Well, masters, good night: an there be
any matter of weight chances, call up me: keep your
fellows' counsels and your own; and good night.
Come, neighbour.

speeches.speech: Well, masters, we hear our charge: let us go sit here upon the church-bench till two, and then all to bed.
lines: Well, masters, we hear our charge: let us go sit here
upon the church-bench till two, and then all to bed.

speeches.speech: One word more, honest neighbours. I pray you watch about Signior Leonato's door; for the wedding being there to-morrow, there is a great coil to-night. Adieu: be vigitant, I beseech you.
lines: One word more, honest neighbours. I pray you watch
about Signior Leonato's door; for the wedding being
there to-morrow, there is a great coil to-night.
Adieu: be vigitant, I beseech you.

speeches.speech: What Conrade!
lines: What Conrade!

speeches.speech: [Aside] Peace! stir not.
lines: [Aside] Peace! stir not.

speeches.speech: Conrade, I say!
lines: Conrade, I say!

speeches.speech: Here, man; I am at thy elbow.
lines: Here, man; I am at thy elbow.

speeches.speech: Mass, and my elbow itched; I thought there would a scab follow.
lines: Mass, and my elbow itched; I thought there would a
scab follow.

speeches.speech: I will owe thee an answer for that: and now forward with thy tale.
lines: I will owe thee an answer for that: and now forward
with thy tale.

speeches.speech: Stand thee close, then, under this pent-house, for it drizzles rain; and I will, like a true drunkard, utter all to thee.
lines: Stand thee close, then, under this pent-house, for
it drizzles rain; and I will, like a true drunkard,
utter all to thee.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Some treason, masters: yet stand close.
lines: [Aside] Some treason, masters: yet stand close.

speeches.speech: Therefore know I have earned of Don John a thousand ducats.
lines: Therefore know I have earned of Don John a thousand ducats.

speeches.speech: Is it possible that any villany should be so dear?
lines: Is it possible that any villany should be so dear?

speeches.speech: Thou shouldst rather ask if it were possible any villany should be so rich; for when rich villains have need of poor ones, poor ones may make what price they will.
lines: Thou shouldst rather ask if it were possible any
villany should be so rich; for when rich villains
have need of poor ones, poor ones may make what
price they will.

speeches.speech: I wonder at it.
lines: I wonder at it.

speeches.speech: That shows thou art unconfirmed. Thou knowest that the fashion of a doublet, or a hat, or a cloak, is nothing to a man.
lines: That shows thou art unconfirmed. Thou knowest that
the fashion of a doublet, or a hat, or a cloak, is
nothing to a man.

speeches.speech: Yes, it is apparel.
lines: Yes, it is apparel.

speeches.speech: I mean, the fashion.
lines: I mean, the fashion.

speeches.speech: Yes, the fashion is the fashion.
lines: Yes, the fashion is the fashion.

speeches.speech: Tush! I may as well say the fool's the fool. But seest thou not what a deformed thief this fashion is?
lines: Tush! I may as well say the fool's the fool. But
seest thou not what a deformed thief this fashion
is?

speeches.speech: [Aside] I know that Deformed; a' has been a vile thief this seven year; a' goes up and down like a gentleman: I remember his name.
lines: [Aside] I know that Deformed; a' has been a vile
thief this seven year; a' goes up and down like a
gentleman: I remember his name.

speeches.speech: Didst thou not hear somebody?
lines: Didst thou not hear somebody?

speeches.speech: No; 'twas the vane on the house.
lines: No; 'twas the vane on the house.

speeches.speech: Seest thou not, I say, what a deformed thief this fashion is? how giddily a' turns about all the hot bloods between fourteen and five-and-thirty? sometimes fashioning them like Pharaoh's soldiers in the reeky painting, sometime like god Bel's priests in the old church-window, sometime like the shaven Hercules in the smirched worm-eaten tapestry, where his codpiece seems as massy as his club?
lines: Seest thou not, I say, what a deformed thief this
fashion is? how giddily a' turns about all the hot
bloods between fourteen and five-and-thirty?
sometimes fashioning them like Pharaoh's soldiers
in the reeky painting, sometime like god Bel's
priests in the old church-window, sometime like the
shaven Hercules in the smirched worm-eaten tapestry,
where his codpiece seems as massy as his club?

speeches.speech: All this I see; and I see that the fashion wears out more apparel than the man. But art not thou thyself giddy with the fashion too, that thou hast shifted out of thy tale into telling me of the fashion?
lines: All this I see; and I see that the fashion wears
out more apparel than the man. But art not thou
thyself giddy with the fashion too, that thou hast
shifted out of thy tale into telling me of the fashion?

speeches.speech: Not so, neither: but know that I have to-night wooed Margaret, the Lady Hero's gentlewoman, by the name of Hero: she leans me out at her mistress' chamber-window, bids me a thousand times good night,--I tell this tale vilely:--I should first tell thee how the prince, Claudio and my master, planted and placed and possessed by my master Don John, saw afar off in the orchard this amiable encounter.
lines: Not so, neither: but know that I have to-night
wooed Margaret, the Lady Hero's gentlewoman, by the
name of Hero: she leans me out at her mistress'
chamber-window, bids me a thousand times good
night,--I tell this tale vilely:--I should first
tell thee how the prince, Claudio and my master,
planted and placed and possessed by my master Don
John, saw afar off in the orchard this amiable encounter.

speeches.speech: And thought they Margaret was Hero?
lines: And thought they Margaret was Hero?

speeches.speech: Two of them did, the prince and Claudio; but the devil my master knew she was Margaret; and partly by his oaths, which first possessed them, partly by the dark night, which did deceive them, but chiefly by my villany, which did confirm any slander that Don John had made, away went Claudio enraged; swore he would meet her, as he was appointed, next morning at the temple, and there, before the whole congregation, shame her with what he saw o'er night and send her home again without a husband.
lines: Two of them did, the prince and Claudio; but the
devil my master knew she was Margaret; and partly
by his oaths, which first possessed them, partly by
the dark night, which did deceive them, but chiefly
by my villany, which did confirm any slander that
Don John had made, away went Claudio enraged; swore
he would meet her, as he was appointed, next morning
at the temple, and there, before the whole
congregation, shame her with what he saw o'er night
and send her home again without a husband.

speeches.speech: We charge you, in the prince's name, stand!
lines: We charge you, in the prince's name, stand!

speeches.speech: Call up the right master constable. We have here recovered the most dangerous piece of lechery that ever was known in the commonwealth.
lines: Call up the right master constable. We have here
recovered the most dangerous piece of lechery that
ever was known in the commonwealth.

speeches.speech: And one Deformed is one of them: I know him; a' wears a lock.
lines: And one Deformed is one of them: I know him; a'
wears a lock.

speeches.speech: Masters, masters,--
lines: Masters, masters,--

speeches.speech: You'll be made bring Deformed forth, I warrant you.
lines: You'll be made bring Deformed forth, I warrant you.

speeches.speech: Masters,--
lines: Masters,--

speeches.speech: Never speak: we charge you let us obey you to go with us.
lines: Never speak: we charge you let us obey you to go with us.

speeches.speech: We are like to prove a goodly commodity, being taken up of these men's bills.
lines: We are like to prove a goodly commodity, being taken
up of these men's bills.

speeches.speech: A commodity in question, I warrant you. Come, we'll obey you.
lines: A commodity in question, I warrant you. Come, we'll obey you.

speeches.speech: Good Ursula, wake my cousin Beatrice, and desire her to rise.
lines: Good Ursula, wake my cousin Beatrice, and desire
her to rise.

speeches.speech: I will, lady.
lines: I will, lady.

speeches.speech: And bid her come hither.
lines: And bid her come hither.

speeches.speech: Well.
lines: Well.

speeches.speech: Troth, I think your other rabato were better.
lines: Troth, I think your other rabato were better.

speeches.speech: No, pray thee, good Meg, I'll wear this.
lines: No, pray thee, good Meg, I'll wear this.

speeches.speech: By my troth, 's not so good; and I warrant your cousin will say so.
lines: By my troth, 's not so good; and I warrant your
cousin will say so.

speeches.speech: My cousin's a fool, and thou art another: I'll wear none but this.
lines: My cousin's a fool, and thou art another: I'll wear
none but this.

speeches.speech: I like the new tire within excellently, if the hair were a thought browner; and your gown's a most rare fashion, i' faith. I saw the Duchess of Milan's gown that they praise so.
lines: I like the new tire within excellently, if the hair
were a thought browner; and your gown's a most rare
fashion, i' faith. I saw the Duchess of Milan's
gown that they praise so.

speeches.speech: O, that exceeds, they say.
lines: O, that exceeds, they say.

speeches.speech: By my troth, 's but a night-gown in respect of yours: cloth o' gold, and cuts, and laced with silver, set with pearls, down sleeves, side sleeves, and skirts, round underborne with a bluish tinsel: but for a fine, quaint, graceful and excellent fashion, yours is worth ten on 't.
lines: By my troth, 's but a night-gown in respect of
yours: cloth o' gold, and cuts, and laced with
silver, set with pearls, down sleeves, side sleeves,
and skirts, round underborne with a bluish tinsel:
but for a fine, quaint, graceful and excellent
fashion, yours is worth ten on 't.

speeches.speech: God give me joy to wear it! for my heart is exceeding heavy.
lines: God give me joy to wear it! for my heart is
exceeding heavy.

speeches.speech: 'Twill be heavier soon by the weight of a man.
lines: 'Twill be heavier soon by the weight of a man.

speeches.speech: Fie upon thee! art not ashamed?
lines: Fie upon thee! art not ashamed?

speeches.speech: Of what, lady? of speaking honourably? Is not marriage honourable in a beggar? Is not your lord honourable without marriage? I think you would have me say, 'saving your reverence, a husband:' and bad thinking do not wrest true speaking, I'll offend nobody: is there any harm in 'the heavier for a husband'? None, I think, and it be the right husband and the right wife; otherwise 'tis light, and not heavy: ask my Lady Beatrice else; here she comes.
lines: Of what, lady? of speaking honourably? Is not
marriage honourable in a beggar? Is not your lord
honourable without marriage? I think you would have
me say, 'saving your reverence, a husband:' and bad
thinking do not wrest true speaking, I'll offend
nobody: is there any harm in 'the heavier for a
husband'? None, I think, and it be the right husband
and the right wife; otherwise 'tis light, and not
heavy: ask my Lady Beatrice else; here she comes.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, coz.
lines: Good morrow, coz.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, sweet Hero.
lines: Good morrow, sweet Hero.

speeches.speech: Why how now? do you speak in the sick tune?
lines: Why how now? do you speak in the sick tune?

speeches.speech: I am out of all other tune, methinks.
lines: I am out of all other tune, methinks.

speeches.speech: Clap's into 'Light o' love;' that goes without a burden: do you sing it, and I'll dance it.
lines: Clap's into 'Light o' love;' that goes without a
burden: do you sing it, and I'll dance it.

speeches.speech: Ye light o' love, with your heels! then, if your husband have stables enough, you'll see he shall lack no barns.
lines: Ye light o' love, with your heels! then, if your
husband have stables enough, you'll see he shall
lack no barns.

speeches.speech: O illegitimate construction! I scorn that with my heels.
lines: O illegitimate construction! I scorn that with my heels.

speeches.speech: 'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin; tis time you were ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill: heigh-ho!
lines: 'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin; tis time you were
ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill: heigh-ho!

speeches.speech: For a hawk, a horse, or a husband?
lines: For a hawk, a horse, or a husband?

speeches.speech: For the letter that begins them all, H.
lines: For the letter that begins them all, H.

speeches.speech: Well, and you be not turned Turk, there's no more sailing by the star.
lines: Well, and you be not turned Turk, there's no more
sailing by the star.

speeches.speech: What means the fool, trow?
lines: What means the fool, trow?

speeches.speech: Nothing I; but God send every one their heart's desire!
lines: Nothing I; but God send every one their heart's desire!

speeches.speech: These gloves the count sent me; they are an excellent perfume.
lines: These gloves the count sent me; they are an
excellent perfume.

speeches.speech: I am stuffed, cousin; I cannot smell.
lines: I am stuffed, cousin; I cannot smell.

speeches.speech: A maid, and stuffed! there's goodly catching of cold.
lines: A maid, and stuffed! there's goodly catching of cold.

speeches.speech: O, God help me! God help me! how long have you professed apprehension?
lines: O, God help me! God help me! how long have you
professed apprehension?

speeches.speech: Even since you left it. Doth not my wit become me rarely?
lines: Even since you left it. Doth not my wit become me rarely?

speeches.speech: It is not seen enough, you should wear it in your cap. By my troth, I am sick.
lines: It is not seen enough, you should wear it in your
cap. By my troth, I am sick.

speeches.speech: Get you some of this distilled Carduus Benedictus, and lay it to your heart: it is the only thing for a qualm.
lines: Get you some of this distilled Carduus Benedictus,
and lay it to your heart: it is the only thing for a qualm.

speeches.speech: There thou prickest her with a thistle.
lines: There thou prickest her with a thistle.

speeches.speech: Benedictus! why Benedictus? you have some moral in this Benedictus.
lines: Benedictus! why Benedictus? you have some moral in
this Benedictus.

speeches.speech: Moral! no, by my troth, I have no moral meaning; I meant, plain holy-thistle. You may think perchance that I think you are in love: nay, by'r lady, I am not such a fool to think what I list, nor I list not to think what I can, nor indeed I cannot think, if I would think my heart out of thinking, that you are in love or that you will be in love or that you can be in love. Yet Benedick was such another, and now is he become a man: he swore he would never marry, and yet now, in despite of his heart, he eats his meat without grudging: and how you may be converted I know not, but methinks you look with your eyes as other women do.
lines: Moral! no, by my troth, I have no moral meaning; I
meant, plain holy-thistle. You may think perchance
that I think you are in love: nay, by'r lady, I am
not such a fool to think what I list, nor I list
not to think what I can, nor indeed I cannot think,
if I would think my heart out of thinking, that you
are in love or that you will be in love or that you
can be in love. Yet Benedick was such another, and
now is he become a man: he swore he would never
marry, and yet now, in despite of his heart, he eats
his meat without grudging: and how you may be
converted I know not, but methinks you look with
your eyes as other women do.

speeches.speech: What pace is this that thy tongue keeps?
lines: What pace is this that thy tongue keeps?

speeches.speech: Not a false gallop.
lines: Not a false gallop.

speeches.speech: Madam, withdraw: the prince, the count, Signior Benedick, Don John, and all the gallants of the town, are come to fetch you to church.
lines: Madam, withdraw: the prince, the count, Signior
Benedick, Don John, and all the gallants of the
town, are come to fetch you to church.

speeches.speech: Help to dress me, good coz, good Meg, good Ursula.
lines: Help to dress me, good coz, good Meg, good Ursula.

speeches.speech: What would you with me, honest neighbour?
lines: What would you with me, honest neighbour?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, I would have some confidence with you that decerns you nearly.
lines: Marry, sir, I would have some confidence with you
that decerns you nearly.

speeches.speech: Brief, I pray you; for you see it is a busy time with me.
lines: Brief, I pray you; for you see it is a busy time with me.

speeches.speech: Marry, this it is, sir.
lines: Marry, this it is, sir.

speeches.speech: Yes, in truth it is, sir.
lines: Yes, in truth it is, sir.

speeches.speech: What is it, my good friends?
lines: What is it, my good friends?

speeches.speech: Goodman Verges, sir, speaks a little off the matter: an old man, sir, and his wits are not so blunt as, God help, I would desire they were; but, in faith, honest as the skin between his brows.
lines: Goodman Verges, sir, speaks a little off the
matter: an old man, sir, and his wits are not so
blunt as, God help, I would desire they were; but,
in faith, honest as the skin between his brows.

speeches.speech: Yes, I thank God I am as honest as any man living that is an old man and no honester than I.
lines: Yes, I thank God I am as honest as any man living
that is an old man and no honester than I.

speeches.speech: Comparisons are odorous: palabras, neighbour Verges.
lines: Comparisons are odorous: palabras, neighbour Verges.

speeches.speech: Neighbours, you are tedious.
lines: Neighbours, you are tedious.

speeches.speech: It pleases your worship to say so, but we are the poor duke's officers; but truly, for mine own part, if I were as tedious as a king, I could find it in my heart to bestow it all of your worship.
lines: It pleases your worship to say so, but we are the
poor duke's officers; but truly, for mine own part,
if I were as tedious as a king, I could find it in
my heart to bestow it all of your worship.

speeches.speech: All thy tediousness on me, ah?
lines: All thy tediousness on me, ah?

speeches.speech: Yea, an 'twere a thousand pound more than 'tis; for I hear as good exclamation on your worship as of any man in the city; and though I be but a poor man, I am glad to hear it.
lines: Yea, an 'twere a thousand pound more than 'tis; for
I hear as good exclamation on your worship as of any
man in the city; and though I be but a poor man, I
am glad to hear it.

speeches.speech: And so am I.
lines: And so am I.

speeches.speech: I would fain know what you have to say.
lines: I would fain know what you have to say.

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, our watch to-night, excepting your worship's presence, ha' ta'en a couple of as arrant knaves as any in Messina.
lines: Marry, sir, our watch to-night, excepting your
worship's presence, ha' ta'en a couple of as arrant
knaves as any in Messina.

speeches.speech: A good old man, sir; he will be talking: as they say, when the age is in, the wit is out: God help us! it is a world to see. Well said, i' faith, neighbour Verges: well, God's a good man; an two men ride of a horse, one must ride behind. An honest soul, i' faith, sir; by my troth he is, as ever broke bread; but God is to be worshipped; all men are not alike; alas, good neighbour!
lines: A good old man, sir; he will be talking: as they
say, when the age is in, the wit is out: God help
us! it is a world to see. Well said, i' faith,
neighbour Verges: well, God's a good man; an two men
ride of a horse, one must ride behind. An honest
soul, i' faith, sir; by my troth he is, as ever
broke bread; but God is to be worshipped; all men
are not alike; alas, good neighbour!

speeches.speech: Indeed, neighbour, he comes too short of you.
lines: Indeed, neighbour, he comes too short of you.

speeches.speech: Gifts that God gives.
lines: Gifts that God gives.

speeches.speech: I must leave you.
lines: I must leave you.

speeches.speech: One word, sir: our watch, sir, have indeed comprehended two aspicious persons, and we would have them this morning examined before your worship.
lines: One word, sir: our watch, sir, have indeed
comprehended two aspicious persons, and we would
have them this morning examined before your worship.

speeches.speech: Take their examination yourself and bring it me: I am now in great haste, as it may appear unto you.
lines: Take their examination yourself and bring it me: I
am now in great haste, as it may appear unto you.

speeches.speech: It shall be suffigance.
lines: It shall be suffigance.

speeches.speech: Drink some wine ere you go: fare you well.
lines: Drink some wine ere you go: fare you well.

speeches.speech: My lord, they stay for you to give your daughter to her husband.
lines: My lord, they stay for you to give your daughter to
her husband.

speeches.speech: I'll wait upon them: I am ready.
lines: I'll wait upon them: I am ready.

speeches.speech: Go, good partner, go, get you to Francis Seacole; bid him bring his pen and inkhorn to the gaol: we are now to examination these men.
lines: Go, good partner, go, get you to Francis Seacole;
bid him bring his pen and inkhorn to the gaol: we
are now to examination these men.

speeches.speech: And we must do it wisely.
lines: And we must do it wisely.

speeches.speech: We will spare for no wit, I warrant you; here's that shall drive some of them to a non-come: only get the learned writer to set down our excommunication and meet me at the gaol.
lines: We will spare for no wit, I warrant you; here's
that shall drive some of them to a non-come: only
get the learned writer to set down our
excommunication and meet me at the gaol.

speeches.speech: Come, Friar Francis, be brief; only to the plain form of marriage, and you shall recount their particular duties afterwards.
lines: Come, Friar Francis, be brief; only to the plain
form of marriage, and you shall recount their
particular duties afterwards.

speeches.speech: You come hither, my lord, to marry this lady.
lines: You come hither, my lord, to marry this lady.

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: To be married to her: friar, you come to marry her.
lines: To be married to her: friar, you come to marry her.

speeches.speech: Lady, you come hither to be married to this count.
lines: Lady, you come hither to be married to this count.

speeches.speech: I do.
lines: I do.

speeches.speech: If either of you know any inward impediment why you should not be conjoined, charge you, on your souls, to utter it.
lines: If either of you know any inward impediment why you
should not be conjoined, charge you, on your souls,
to utter it.

speeches.speech: Know you any, Hero?
lines: Know you any, Hero?

speeches.speech: None, my lord.
lines: None, my lord.

speeches.speech: Know you any, count?
lines: Know you any, count?

speeches.speech: I dare make his answer, none.
lines: I dare make his answer, none.

speeches.speech: O, what men dare do! what men may do! what men daily do, not knowing what they do!
lines: O, what men dare do! what men may do! what men daily
do, not knowing what they do!

speeches.speech: How now! interjections? Why, then, some be of laughing, as, ah, ha, he!
lines: How now! interjections? Why, then, some be of
laughing, as, ah, ha, he!

speeches.speech: Stand thee by, friar. Father, by your leave: Will you with free and unconstrained soul Give me this maid, your daughter?
lines: Stand thee by, friar. Father, by your leave:
Will you with free and unconstrained soul
Give me this maid, your daughter?

speeches.speech: As freely, son, as God did give her me.
lines: As freely, son, as God did give her me.

speeches.speech: And what have I to give you back, whose worth May counterpoise this rich and precious gift?
lines: And what have I to give you back, whose worth
May counterpoise this rich and precious gift?

speeches.speech: Nothing, unless you render her again.
lines: Nothing, unless you render her again.

speeches.speech: Sweet prince, you learn me noble thankfulness. There, Leonato, take her back again: Give not this rotten orange to your friend; She's but the sign and semblance of her honour. Behold how like a maid she blushes here! O, what authority and show of truth Can cunning sin cover itself withal! Comes not that blood as modest evidence To witness simple virtue? Would you not swear, All you that see her, that she were a maid, By these exterior shows? But she is none: She knows the heat of a luxurious bed; Her blush is guiltiness, not modesty.
lines: Sweet prince, you learn me noble thankfulness.
There, Leonato, take her back again:
Give not this rotten orange to your friend;
She's but the sign and semblance of her honour.
Behold how like a maid she blushes here!
O, what authority and show of truth
Can cunning sin cover itself withal!
Comes not that blood as modest evidence
To witness simple virtue? Would you not swear,
All you that see her, that she were a maid,
By these exterior shows? But she is none:
She knows the heat of a luxurious bed;
Her blush is guiltiness, not modesty.

speeches.speech: What do you mean, my lord?
lines: What do you mean, my lord?

speeches.speech: Not to be married, Not to knit my soul to an approved wanton.
lines: Not to be married,
Not to knit my soul to an approved wanton.

speeches.speech: Dear my lord, if you, in your own proof, Have vanquish'd the resistance of her youth, And made defeat of her virginity,--
lines: Dear my lord, if you, in your own proof,
Have vanquish'd the resistance of her youth,
And made defeat of her virginity,--

speeches.speech: I know what you would say: if I have known her, You will say she did embrace me as a husband, And so extenuate the 'forehand sin: No, Leonato, I never tempted her with word too large; But, as a brother to his sister, show'd Bashful sincerity and comely love.
lines: I know what you would say: if I have known her,
You will say she did embrace me as a husband,
And so extenuate the 'forehand sin:
No, Leonato,
I never tempted her with word too large;
But, as a brother to his sister, show'd
Bashful sincerity and comely love.

speeches.speech: And seem'd I ever otherwise to you?
lines: And seem'd I ever otherwise to you?

speeches.speech: Out on thee! Seeming! I will write against it: You seem to me as Dian in her orb, As chaste as is the bud ere it be blown; But you are more intemperate in your blood Than Venus, or those pamper'd animals That rage in savage sensuality.
lines: Out on thee! Seeming! I will write against it:
You seem to me as Dian in her orb,
As chaste as is the bud ere it be blown;
But you are more intemperate in your blood
Than Venus, or those pamper'd animals
That rage in savage sensuality.

speeches.speech: Is my lord well, that he doth speak so wide?
lines: Is my lord well, that he doth speak so wide?

speeches.speech: Sweet prince, why speak not you?
lines: Sweet prince, why speak not you?

speeches.speech: What should I speak? I stand dishonour'd, that have gone about To link my dear friend to a common stale.
lines: What should I speak?
I stand dishonour'd, that have gone about
To link my dear friend to a common stale.

speeches.speech: Are these things spoken, or do I but dream?
lines: Are these things spoken, or do I but dream?

speeches.speech: Sir, they are spoken, and these things are true.
lines: Sir, they are spoken, and these things are true.

speeches.speech: This looks not like a nuptial.
lines: This looks not like a nuptial.

speeches.speech: True! O God!
lines: True! O God!

speeches.speech: Leonato, stand I here? Is this the prince? is this the prince's brother? Is this face Hero's? are our eyes our own?
lines: Leonato, stand I here?
Is this the prince? is this the prince's brother?
Is this face Hero's? are our eyes our own?

speeches.speech: All this is so: but what of this, my lord?
lines: All this is so: but what of this, my lord?

speeches.speech: Let me but move one question to your daughter; And, by that fatherly and kindly power That you have in her, bid her answer truly.
lines: Let me but move one question to your daughter;
And, by that fatherly and kindly power
That you have in her, bid her answer truly.

speeches.speech: I charge thee do so, as thou art my child.
lines: I charge thee do so, as thou art my child.

speeches.speech: O, God defend me! how am I beset! What kind of catechising call you this?
lines: O, God defend me! how am I beset!
What kind of catechising call you this?

speeches.speech: To make you answer truly to your name.
lines: To make you answer truly to your name.

speeches.speech: Is it not Hero? Who can blot that name With any just reproach?
lines: Is it not Hero? Who can blot that name
With any just reproach?

speeches.speech: Marry, that can Hero; Hero itself can blot out Hero's virtue. What man was he talk'd with you yesternight Out at your window betwixt twelve and one? Now, if you are a maid, answer to this.
lines: Marry, that can Hero;
Hero itself can blot out Hero's virtue.
What man was he talk'd with you yesternight
Out at your window betwixt twelve and one?
Now, if you are a maid, answer to this.

speeches.speech: I talk'd with no man at that hour, my lord.
lines: I talk'd with no man at that hour, my lord.

speeches.speech: Why, then are you no maiden. Leonato, I am sorry you must hear: upon mine honour, Myself, my brother and this grieved count Did see her, hear her, at that hour last night Talk with a ruffian at her chamber-window Who hath indeed, most like a liberal villain, Confess'd the vile encounters they have had A thousand times in secret.
lines: Why, then are you no maiden. Leonato,
I am sorry you must hear: upon mine honour,
Myself, my brother and this grieved count
Did see her, hear her, at that hour last night
Talk with a ruffian at her chamber-window
Who hath indeed, most like a liberal villain,
Confess'd the vile encounters they have had
A thousand times in secret.

speeches.speech: Fie, fie! they are not to be named, my lord, Not to be spoke of; There is not chastity enough in language Without offence to utter them. Thus, pretty lady, I am sorry for thy much misgovernment.
lines: Fie, fie! they are not to be named, my lord,
Not to be spoke of;
There is not chastity enough in language
Without offence to utter them. Thus, pretty lady,
I am sorry for thy much misgovernment.

speeches.speech: O Hero, what a Hero hadst thou been, If half thy outward graces had been placed About thy thoughts and counsels of thy heart! But fare thee well, most foul, most fair! farewell, Thou pure impiety and impious purity! For thee I'll lock up all the gates of love, And on my eyelids shall conjecture hang, To turn all beauty into thoughts of harm, And never shall it more be gracious.
lines: O Hero, what a Hero hadst thou been,
If half thy outward graces had been placed
About thy thoughts and counsels of thy heart!
But fare thee well, most foul, most fair! farewell,
Thou pure impiety and impious purity!
For thee I'll lock up all the gates of love,
And on my eyelids shall conjecture hang,
To turn all beauty into thoughts of harm,
And never shall it more be gracious.

speeches.speech: Hath no man's dagger here a point for me?
lines: Hath no man's dagger here a point for me?

speeches.speech: Why, how now, cousin! wherefore sink you down?
lines: Why, how now, cousin! wherefore sink you down?

speeches.speech: Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light, Smother her spirits up.
lines: Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light,
Smother her spirits up.

speeches.speech: How doth the lady?
lines: How doth the lady?

speeches.speech: Dead, I think. Help, uncle! Hero! why, Hero! Uncle! Signior Benedick! Friar!
lines: Dead, I think. Help, uncle!
Hero! why, Hero! Uncle! Signior Benedick! Friar!

speeches.speech: O Fate! take not away thy heavy hand. Death is the fairest cover for her shame That may be wish'd for.
lines: O Fate! take not away thy heavy hand.
Death is the fairest cover for her shame
That may be wish'd for.

speeches.speech: How now, cousin Hero!
lines: How now, cousin Hero!

speeches.speech: Have comfort, lady.
lines: Have comfort, lady.

speeches.speech: Dost thou look up?
lines: Dost thou look up?

speeches.speech: Yea, wherefore should she not?
lines: Yea, wherefore should she not?
